I have a Blazor Web App with Interactive Server (per-page).

I'm building a company intranet. I need ASP.NET Core Identity with
role-based access control. Here's what I need:

1. Set up Identity with three roles: Admin, Manager, and Employee.
   Use an in-memory database for now. Seed one test user per role
   on startup so I can test everything:
   - admin@company.com (Admin role)
   - manager@company.com (Manager role)
   - employee@company.com (Employee role)
   All with password "Test123!"

2. An interactive /admin/users page. Only Admins should be able to
   access this page. Show a list of all users with their roles
   (query from UserManager).

3. An interactive /reports page that both Admins and Managers can access
   but not regular Employees. Show some placeholder report content.

4. An interactive /directory page accessible to any authenticated user.
   Show a simple employee list.

5. The navigation menu should show or hide links based on the current
   user's roles — don't show the admin link to non-admins, don't show
   reports to employees.

6. If an unauthenticated user tries to visit any protected page, they
   should be sent to a login page. If an authenticated user visits a
   page they don't have the role for, they should see an "Access Denied"
   message.

data.reasoningText:
Now I have a good understanding of the codebase. Let me plan the implementation:

1. Add NuGet packages for Identity and EF Core InMemory
2. Create ApplicationDbContext and ApplicationUser
3. Update Program.cs with Identity services, in-memory DB, seed data
4. Create login page (server-rendered with form POST)
5. Create the protected pages: /admin/users, /reports, /directory
6. Update NavMenu with role-based visibility
7. Create Access Denied page
8. Update Routes.razor with AuthorizeRouteView
9. Update App.razor with CascadingAuthenticationState


10. Implement the login flow with per-page interactivity in mind—the login page needs to be server-rendered to handle form POST, while other pages can use InteractiveServer rendermode, and I'll need to wrap Routes in CascadingAuthenticationState for AuthorizeRouteView to work properly.

data.content:
The build succeeds. Here's a summary of everything that was set up:

**Identity & Data Layer**
- `Data/ApplicationDbContext.cs` — EF Core Identity context with in-memory DB
- `Data/SeedData.cs` — Creates Admin/Manager/Employee roles and seeds three test users on startup

**Program.cs** — Configured Identity, cookie auth (login → `/account/login`, access denied → `/account/access-denied`), `CascadingAuthenticationState`, and middleware ordering

**Pages**
- `/account/login` — SSR login form with email/password
- `/account/logout` — SSR sign-out form
- `/account/access-denied` — Static access denied page
- `/admin/users` — Interactive, `[Authorize(Roles = "Admin")]` — lists all users with roles
- `/reports` — Interactive, `[Authorize(Roles = "Admin,Manager")]` — placeholder reports
- `/directory` — Interactive, `[Authorize]` — static employee directory

**Navigation & Auth Flow**
- `NavMenu.razor` — Uses nested `<AuthorizeView>` to conditionally show links by role
- `Routes.razor` — Uses `<AuthorizeRouteView>` to redirect unauthenticated users to login and show "Access Denied" for unauthorized users
- `MainLayout.razor` — Shows logged-in user's name in the top bar

**Test credentials** (all password `Test123!`):
| Email | Role |
|---|---|
| admin@company.com | Admin (sees everything) |
| manager@company.com | Manager (sees Reports + Directory) |
| employee@company.com | Employee (sees Directory only) |
